episode_title: Kasper Lindskow: Building a Scalable AI Infrastructure at Denmark's JP/Politikens Media Group
transcript_file: 2025-03-18 - Kasper Lindskow Building a Scalable AI Infrastructure at Denmark's JPPolitikens Media Group.txt
summary_claims: Two recommender systems with the same click rate or sales performance can have very different effects on the news flow, e.g., one biasing toward popular/entertainment content and the other toward niche/long-tail stories
Hallucinations and factuality mistakes, initially expected to be the biggest challenge with generative AI, turned out to be relatively rare when using direct prompting and RAG setups
The real challenge with generative AI output was not factuality but blandness, repetitiveness, and lack of brand-specific voice adaptation
In-housing recommender systems yields more commercial value (engagement, signups) because effective personalization requires customization and adaptation to brand-specific data

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: Even though you might find the first output you saw interesting to read, once you read five of those, it would be too repetitive and boring and not really fitting to each of the news brands.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Magna provides writing assistance (proofreading, translation, headline suggestions, article criticism) as the most-used generative tools. The episode reveals the real challenge is not factuality but blandness and lack of brand voice, underscoring why human editorial input remains essential. Translation is treated as a mature use case; draft generation requires significant human refinement.
matches.matched_text: even though you might find the first output you saw interesting to read, once you read uh five of those, it would be too repetitive and boring and not really fitting to each of the news brands.

matches.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: We really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Trustworthiness drives technical priorities -- factuality testing was the first concern. Recommender systems' differential effects on news flow (overrepresenting entertainment vs. promoting diversity) directly impact the audience trust contract. The episode shows that engagement metrics alone are insufficient; editorial impact on the news mix must be monitored.
matches.matched_text: we really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.

matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: You should still make sure that you really understand the technology well enough... so that you can have an informed dialogue with your vendor... because you need to be able to ask that vendor the right questions and make the right demands.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Different editorial brands set their own AI guidelines rather than enforcing uniform policies, reflecting a principles-over-prescriptive-rules approach. Bias in metadata classification when moving from fine-tuned models to generative models is a concrete ethics concern. Responsible procurement is addressed through the advice that even buyers must understand the technology deeply.
matches.matched_text: you should still make sure that you really understand the technology well enough That requires investing some time and some trial and error in it so that you can have an informed dialogue with your vendor, with your CMS system, or your or your supplier of recommender systems, or whatever it might be, because you need to be able to ask that vendor the right questions and make the right demands on it to make sure that the tools you get can uh either be directly adapted to your values and your profile as a publisher or that you at least have tools to monitor.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: Two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales... have very different effects on the news flow. And that's sort of the core of your profile, right?
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Deeply technical: fine-tuned Danish transformer models for metadata classification; RAG-based research tools; collaborative filtering and content-based recommender systems; embedding models for semantic similarity; tone-of-voice fine-tuning limited to open-source models; prompt chaining and agents as next step beyond prompting limits. Multi-brand architecture requires flexible infrastructure enabling/disabling tools per brand.
matches.matched_text: two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales or whatever you might mention it on in those hardcore metrics and have very different effects on the news flow.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: We shouldn't, in my sort of experience and opinion, make all journalists prompt engineers.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Key design philosophy: do not make all journalists into prompt engineers; build focused tools with specialized prompting underneath. Addresses the two-tier adoption divide between AI front-runners comfortable with chat interfaces and average journalists who need button/dropdown UX. Change management is described as linear even when technology is exponential.
matches.matched_text: we shouldn't, in my sort of experience and and and opinion, make all journalists prompt engineers.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: Even if the technology develops exponentially, then rolling it out and adapting it, that's change management. That's a very linear process.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Most organizationally rich episode: centralized AI unit vs. local AI hubs; build in-house for core editorial functions, buy for generic business; values compass framework balancing journalistic, technical, business, and user considerations; started with applied research via university partnerships and public funding; transition from building for front-runners to building for the average journalist. 2025 is positioned as the big rollout year.
matches.matched_text: Even if the technology is developed develops exponentially. Then uh rolling it out and adapting it, that's change management. That's a very linear process.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: You should still make sure that you really understand the technology well enough... so that you can have an informed dialogue with your vendor.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Tangentially present through the build-vs-buy discussion and the need for publishers to maintain enough technical literacy to hold vendors accountable. No substantive discussion of platform power dynamics, content licensing, or traffic collapse.
matches.matched_text: you should still make sure that you really understand the technology well enough That requires investing some time and some trial and error in it so that you can have an informed dialogue with your vendor, with your CMS system, or your or your supplier of recommender systems, or whatever it might be, because you need to be able to ask that vendor the right questions and make the right demands on it to make sure that the tools you get can uh either be directly adapted to your values and your profile as a publisher or that you at least have tools to monitor.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: Two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales... have very different effects on the news flow.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Recommender systems on Ekstra Bladet's front page are a core production system driving engagement, signups, and sales. AI-generated illustrations are used at select brands. RAG-based summarization across article archives creates new content formats (fact boxes, FAQs, quizzes). The episode deeply explores how personalization engines must be editorially constrained to avoid distorting the news mix.
matches.matched_text: two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales or whatever you might mention it on in those hardcore metrics and have very different effects on the news flow.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: Two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales... have very different effects on the news flow.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: In-house recommender systems drive commercial value (engagement, signups, sales). Started with applied research funded by Innovation Fund Denmark before committing internal budgets. The data asset (25-year archive, brand-specific corpora) is treated as a competitive advantage that commodity AI cannot replicate.
matches.matched_text: two recommenders that have the same ability to engage or drive sales or whatever you might mention it on in those hardcore metrics and have very different effects on the news flow.

matches.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: We really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Regulation is not directly discussed. The episode touches on editorial constraints on recommender systems (e.g., caps on topic overrepresentation, excluding sensitive content) which function as internal governance, but formal legal or regulatory frameworks are absent.
matches.matched_text: we really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: Even if the technology develops exponentially, then rolling it out and adapting it, that's change management. That's a very linear process.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Multiple tools in production (Magna, recommenders, metadata classifiers); the transition from AI front-runner tools to average-journalist tools represents a scaling challenge. Two UX paradigms (chat vs. buttons) reflect the adoption engineering problem. The prototype-to-production gap is framed as a change management bottleneck rather than a technical one.
matches.matched_text: Even if the technology is developed develops exponentially. Then uh rolling it out and adapting it, that's change management. That's a very linear process.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: We really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.
best_quote.speaker: Kasper Lindskow

matches.summary: Data quality is implicitly important -- fine-tuned classification models require test sets for bias control, and the Magna platform operates within editorial policy to keep data secure. No explicit discussion of source protection or privacy frameworks.
matches.matched_text: we really don't like those factual mistakes, right? We should be trustworthy and we shouldn't be making those mistakes.
